name: Standardized Commit Messages with Conventional Commits
description: Set up and enforce the Conventional Commits specification with commitlint and commitizen for consistent, parseable commit messages.
trendingReason: Conventional Commits are now a prerequisite for automated semantic versioning and changelog generation in modern CI/CD pipelines.
prerequisites: Basic understanding of commit message conventions

steps.title: Install commitlint and the conventional config
steps.description: Install commitlint, which validates commit messages, along with the conventional commits preset that defines the rules.

steps.title: Create the commitlint configuration file
steps.description: Create a config file that tells commitlint to use the conventional commits ruleset. Types include feat, fix, docs, style, refactor, test, chore, and more.
steps.tip: You can customize rules by adding a 'rules' object to the config. For example, set max header length or allow custom types.

steps.title: Install husky for Git hooks
steps.command: npm install --save-dev husky && npx husky init
steps.description: Install husky and initialize it. This creates a .husky directory with a pre-commit hook template.

steps.title: Add a commit-msg hook for commitlint
steps.description: Create a commit-msg hook that runs commitlint against every commit message. Invalid messages will be rejected.
steps.warning: Make sure the hook file is executable. On Unix systems, run 'chmod +x .husky/commit-msg'.

steps.title: Test with a valid conventional commit
steps.command: git commit --allow-empty -m "feat: add user authentication module"
steps.description: Create a test commit using the conventional format: type(optional-scope): description. The hook should pass.
steps.tip: Common types: feat (new feature), fix (bug fix), docs (documentation), refactor (code restructuring), test (adding tests), chore (maintenance).

steps.title: Test with an invalid commit message
steps.description: Try committing with a non-conventional message. Commitlint should reject it with an error explaining the required format.

fullScript:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# conventional-commits-setup.sh — Set up commitlint + husky
set -euo pipefail

echo "Installing commitlint..."
npm install --save-dev @commitlint/cli @commitlint/config-conventional

echo ""
echo "Creating commitlint config..."
cat > commitlint.config.mjs << 'CONFIGEOF'
export default {
  extends: ['@commitlint/config-conventional'],
  rules: {
    'header-max-length': [2, 'always', 100],
    'type-enum': [
      2,
      'always',
      [
        'feat', 'fix', 'docs', 'style', 'refactor',
        'perf', 'test', 'build', 'ci', 'chore', 'revert',
      ],
    ],
  },
};
CONFIGEOF

echo ""
echo "Installing and initializing husky..."
npm install --save-dev husky
npx husky init

echo ""
echo "Creating commit-msg hook..."
echo 'npx --no -- commitlint --edit "$1"' > .husky/commit-msg
chmod +x .husky/commit-msg 2>/dev/null || true

echo ""
echo "Setup complete. Conventional commit format:"
echo "  type(scope): description"
echo ""
echo "Examples:"
echo "  feat(auth): add OAuth2 login flow"
echo "  fix(api): handle null response from payment gateway"
echo "  docs: update API reference for v3 endpoints"

faq.question: What are the standard Conventional Commit types?
faq.answer: The most common types are: feat (new feature), fix (bug fix), docs (documentation), style (formatting), refactor (code restructuring without behavior change), perf (performance), test (adding/fixing tests), build (build system changes), ci (CI config), chore (maintenance), and revert (reverting a commit).

faq.question: How do Conventional Commits enable automated versioning?
faq.answer: Tools like semantic-release parse commit types to determine version bumps: feat triggers a minor version bump, fix triggers a patch bump, and a BREAKING CHANGE footer triggers a major bump. This enables fully automated semver releases.

faq.question: Can I add a custom commit type like 'wip'?
faq.answer: Yes. Add a 'type-enum' rule to your commitlint config that includes your custom type in the allowed list. However, keep in mind that non-standard types will not be recognized by tools like semantic-release unless you configure them separately.
